assignment_description:
➜ NOTE TO STUDENTS
One purpose of composition lessons is to encourage you, the student, to dig deeper, reflect on your readings, explore the different ways words work, and learn how to ask good questions of yourself and your readings. Set your timer and use the whole time allotted for a composition assignment. If you finish an assignment with extra time, explore another perspective, ask further questions, or take a bit more time to see if any other thoughts come to mind. Alternatively, if you find yourself stuck, you may want to take a short break and return later. Growth, not frustration, is the goal.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Summary
Write a brief summary of a current event.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Descriptive
Describe the village of Grand-Pré from Evangeline. 
OR 
Describe the land of Greece.

• REFERENCES
Comp #2
Poetry Reading (G7) 
(Lessons 3-4)
Book: Evangeline
OR 
Ancient History (G7-9) 
(Lesson 1)
Book: Ancient Greek World

Make sure students are practicing typing skills and typing narrations, and compositions regularly.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Expository/Research
(US Students) What were some of the issues the new country had to deal with? Why was a new governing document needed?"
OR
(Canadian Students) Hudson Bay Company recently filed for creditor protection and began closing and liquidating (selling off the assets) of all its stores, effectively closing the business. There is currently (April 2025) a debate over whether or not the company should be allowed to auction off the charter to recoup some funds. This is a great opportunity to think about how events of long-ago history can continue to be felt in the present day. With a parent's supervision, research what has happened to the charter since April 2025 and decide which side of the debate you would support.

Alternate Option: From your reading in Ourselves, what are ways a flame could come from a foreign land?

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Opinion

⬚ 20m Grammar/Comp: Grade 7 - Lesson 19
Composition

➜ PRACTICE
Reread the sentences on each of your note cards from last time. On the back of each card, write the subject and the verb. This is the essence of the sentence. Any other words you use should enhance that essence and not detract from it. Now write any words and phrases you used to modify the subject and verb. Are they all necessary? Should more be added for clarity? Could some less interesting words be replaced by better ones? List any new word or phrase ideas with the word they modify.

⬚ 20m Grammar/Comp: Grade 7 - Lesson 20
Composition

➜ PRACTICE
Look again at the back side of your note cards. On the lined side, rewrite each sentence using the best words from the back. Play with the word order. Keep rearranging until you have maximum clarity and impact. Make sure all your modifiers are in the right places! Think about sound. Are there words that would enhance the beauty of each sentence? Go ahead and use them. Rewrite as many times as you need to.

What do you notice about the different ways the authors begin and end the story? What would you think each story was about if you just read those opening and closing lines? Jot down your thoughts.

⬚ 20m Grammar/Comp: Grade 7 - Lesson 21
Composition

➜ PRACTICE
Now that each sentence is clear, correct, and beautiful, play with ordering the note cards. You might start by making stacks of sentences that belong together and then deciding on the order of the stacks. Keep rearranging your note cards until they are in a logical order that will make sense to the reader. If you find that you need another sentence in order for things to make the most sense, go ahead and craft one on another note card.

⬚ 20m Grammar/Comp: Grade 7 - Lesson 22
Composition

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the final version of each sentence, in the final order, from your notecards into another draft of your paragraph. Read the original and the second drafts aloud. How much of a difference is there? As you reread the second draft, do you notice things you would change? Then change them! This is what authors do to make their work the best it can be. Reread one last time to check for capitals, punctuation, spelling, indentation, etc., and make any corrections necessary.

assignment_description:
➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Short Account
From your reading in Ourselves, tell how to keep Smell "in good working order."

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Editing
Editing is an important aspect of writing, especially when sharing it with others.

• Look back at one of your old composition assignments from Term 1.
• Type it up (if it isn't already).
• Read it aloud and fix any errors you hear as you read.
• Now, using what you've learned in Paragraph Town, review your sentences. Are they complete sentences? Do the subjects and verbs agree? Check your punctuation and capitalization. Fix any other errors you find!

Remember: all good writers edit their work. It's a good thing to find and fix errors! Very few people write perfect sentences the first time they put their ideas on paper (or type them).

• REFERENCES
Comp #1
Ethics: Part 1 (Lesson 13)
Book: Ourselves

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Summary
Write a summary of a current event.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Personification
You might have noticed that Benjamin Franklin's "wit and wisdom" in Poetry Reading is filled with personification. An author uses personification when s/he gives an inanimate object or abstract idea the qualities of a person. For instance, here is one of Franklin's aphorisms:

"Anger and Folly walk cheek by-jole; 
Repentance treads on both their Heels."

Notice how Franklin gives Anger, Folly, and Repentance human-like qualities. They are all walking! Look for more instances of personification in the books you read. With the remaining time, try writing a few sentences like Franklin's using personification. For instance, start by trying to write the opposite of Franklin's saying!

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1-2 - Extended Reflection
Spend both your composition periods working on one of these longer assignments.

Option 1: (Canadian Students) Continue your writing exercise from Lesson 33.
OR
Option 2: In Much Ado About Nothing, Act 3, Scene 2, Don John tricks Claudio into believing that Hero is unfaithful. Imagine you have overheard Don John’s scheme before Claudio falls for the lie. Your task is to write a formal letter warning Claudio not to trust Don John and to have him arrested by the Night Watch led by Dogberry.

Your Task: Write a formal letter to Claudio, urging him not to trust Don John. Your letter should include:
\- A proper address (e.g., "Dear Claudio")
\- A clear introduction stating your purpose
\- Reasons why he should not trust Don John, including: Actions or quotes from Don John revealing his schemes
\- Why Hero is trustworthy and why he should marry her
\- A closing and signature (e.g., "Sincerely, [Your Name]")

Your letter should be at least one to two paragraphs.

• REFERENCE
Comp #1
Canadian History (G7-8, Lesson 33)

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Scene

Remember that plays essentially rely on dialogue and stage directions to work. So it may even help to try acting it out or imagining how you'd set it up in a room first. Feel free to look back at your books and use excerpts of dialogue from the book as you work on your scene.

OR

We can use the same questions for books that you might use for nature study to make observations about books and generate more questions: "What do you notice?" "What do you wonder?" and "What does it remind you of?" Don't worry if you think of a lot of questions and then draw a blank. Give yourself some time and keep thinking, and you'll probably come up with some new questions!

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #1 - Editing
Pick one of your favorite comparison assignments from Term 1 or 2. Today we're going to practice what you've been learning in Paragraph Town to revise your work.
• Type it if it isn't already typed. 
• Read your work aloud and fix any initial grammar or spelling mistakes you see/hear. 
• Use what you've learned from grammar to check for any other errors to correct (subject/verb, sentence fragments, etc.)
• Review comparison paragraphs and the different types of paragraphs in Paragraph Town. 
• Now look back at your writing and see what you can do to make it an even clearer comparison paragraph. Do you have a topic sentence or connecting words? Do you have enough sentences in your paragraph? Try rearranging the sentences in a different order (you could physically write or print them out and cut them apart, and try rearranging if you like). What order do you think might give the most natural progression of thought? Feel free to add or take away words, phrases, or sentences. Now is the time to revise, and often writers only keep a little bit of their initial work when they revisit it! That doesn't mean your first work was bad; you just know more and have new thoughts on how to make it better now that you've given it some time to rest!
• If you need more time, continue this exercise during Composition assignment #2 this week.

➜ ⬚ 15m COMPOSITION ASSIGNMENT #2 - Summary

OR

➜ PRACTICE
Narration Paragraph 3: Look at your outline. Are you still wondering the same things you were when you wrote it? If your thoughts have changed, replace them. The assignment specifically asks you to tell what you found most interesting and asks what you want to know more about, but try to write without using first person. Instead of "I want to know more about..." try, "It would be interesting to learn more about..."

Write your third paragraph.

⬚ 20m Grammar/Comp: Grade 7 - Lesson 70
Composition

➜ PRACTICE
Reread your three paragraphs. Do your topic sentences connect your paragraphs? Is everything in a logical order? Did you choose words for clarity, accuracy, and beauty? Edit for spelling, punctuation, capitalization, indentation, etc.

Write your final draft.
